methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $100M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Housing & Construction (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 1 same-sector peers exist in Housing & Construction, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: Cuby Technologies
private.rationale: Same sector (Housing & Construction), same stage (Series B)

data.narrative:
Cover, a Series B-stage housing and construction company founded by Alexis Rivas, has raised over $100M to date. The relevant comp universe in Housing & Construction is sparse, consisting of two private peers (ICON and Cuby Technologies) with no public comparables available. The narrow peer set precludes a reliable median-based valuation approach, as same-stage and adjacent-stage Housing & Construction comparables fall short of the three-datapoint minimum required for statistical relevance.

The Stage-Progression method is the applicable triangulation approach here. Using the $100M in total raised as a valuation proxy and applying a 1.9x step-up multiplier typical of Series B advancement, this method implies a valuation of $190M. This reflects the expected value increase associated with progression toward later funding rounds.

The analysis supports a median valuation of $190M, with a range of $162M (low) to $218M (high) reflecting ±15% variance around the central estimate. The primary uncertainty stems from the absence of robust peer-level benchmarking in this sector; a durable valuation would benefit from either expanding the Housing & Construction comp set or stress-testing the Stage-Progression multiplier against historical progression patterns in adjacent sectors with deeper public market data. Current-stage revenue, growth trajectory, and capital efficiency metrics would further inform the upper and lower bounds of this range.
